facts.size_shape: One of North America's largest raptors, 28-38 inches long with a massive 6-8 foot wingspan. Broad flat wings, a large hooked yellow bill, and powerful talons.
facts.color_pattern: Adults have a striking white head and tail with a dark brown body. Juveniles are mottled brown and white, not gaining the full white head until around age 5.
facts.where_to_find: Found throughout North America near large bodies of open water — rivers, lakes, reservoirs, and coastlines. Most abundant in Alaska, Canada, and the Pacific Northwest.
facts.what_they_eat: Primarily fish snatched live from the water, plus waterfowl, small mammals, and carrion.
facts.behavior: Often seen soaring high on flat wings or perched in tall trees overlooking water. Famous for pirating food from Ospreys mid-air. Builds enormous stick nests up to 10 feet wide, reused for decades.

facts.size_shape: Tiny, round songbird about 5 inches long with a plump body, big round head, short neck, and a long narrow tail.
facts.color_pattern: Black cap and bib, white cheeks, soft gray back and wings, and buffy sides. The contrasting black-and-white head pattern is unmistakable.
facts.where_to_find: Found year-round across northern North America from Alaska to the northeastern US and along the Rockies. Thrives in forests, parks, and suburban yards.
facts.what_they_eat: Seeds, berries, and insects. A regular at backyard feeders, especially for sunflower seeds and suet.
facts.behavior: Famously curious and tame — will sometimes eat from a hand. Known for its cheerful 'chick-a-dee-dee-dee' call; more 'dee' notes signal greater alarm. Caches food to retrieve in winter.

facts.size_shape: A large goose, 30-43 inches long with a wingspan up to 5.5 feet. Long neck, large body, and a broad flat bill.
facts.color_pattern: Black head and neck with a distinctive white chin strap patch. Brown back and wings with a pale gray-brown breast and white undertail.
facts.where_to_find: Found throughout North America near water — parks, golf courses, lakes, rivers, and farmland. A year-round resident across much of the US.
facts.what_they_eat: Grasses, grains, aquatic plants, and occasionally berries. Grazes heavily on lawns and farm fields.
facts.behavior: Flies in characteristic V-formations while honking loudly. Pairs mate for life and will hiss and charge aggressively at intruders near a nest.

facts.size_shape: A medium-sized diving duck, 15-20 inches long with a large, rounded puffy head and a short bill. Compact and stocky on the water.
facts.color_pattern: Males have an iridescent green-black head with a round white spot near the bill and a bold black-and-white body. Females have a chocolate-brown head, gray body, and yellow-tipped bill.
facts.where_to_find: Breeds in boreal forests of Canada and Alaska near lakes and rivers. Winters across much of the US on coastal bays, rivers, and lakes.
facts.what_they_eat: Aquatic invertebrates, crustaceans, and small fish, caught by diving.
facts.behavior: Named for its bright golden-yellow eye. Males perform an elaborate head-throwing courtship display in winter. Wings produce a distinctive loud whistling sound in flight.

facts.size_shape: A large, slender diving duck, 22-27 inches long with a long body, narrow serrated bill, and a distinctive crested head. Sits low in the water.
facts.color_pattern: Males have a glossy dark green head, bright red serrated bill, white body, and black back. Females have a rusty-red crested head, gray body, and a clean white chin patch.
facts.where_to_find: Found across North America on clear freshwater rivers, lakes, and reservoirs. Breeds in forested areas near water; winters on open inland waters and along coasts.
facts.what_they_eat: Primarily fish, caught by diving and chasing underwater. The serrated bill helps grip slippery prey.
facts.behavior: Often seen diving repeatedly in fast-moving rivers. Females lead large broods of ducklings, which sometimes pile onto her back. Flies low and fast over water.

facts.size_shape: A medium-sized dabbling duck, 18-22 inches long. Slimmer and more elegant than a Mallard with a steep forehead and thin bill.
facts.color_pattern: Males are intricately patterned gray and brown with a black rear end and a white wing patch visible in flight. Females are mottled brown like a female Mallard but have an orange-sided bill.
facts.where_to_find: Found across North America, breeding in prairie wetlands and wintering along both coasts and in the south. Prefers freshwater marshes and ponds with dense vegetation.
facts.what_they_eat: Mostly aquatic plants, seeds, and algae. Occasionally steals food from diving ducks.
facts.behavior: A quiet, understated duck often overlooked among more colorful species. The white wing patch is the key field mark to look for in flight or on the water.

facts.size_shape: The smallest dabbling duck in North America at only 12-15 inches long. Very compact and fast-flying with rapid wingbeats.
facts.color_pattern: Males have a chestnut head with a broad iridescent green eye patch, finely barred gray body, and a bright yellow patch under the tail. Females are mottled brown similar to other female dabbling ducks.
facts.where_to_find: Breeds across Canada and Alaska; winters across the southern US, both coasts, and into Central America on marshes and shallow wetlands.
facts.what_they_eat: Seeds, aquatic plants, and invertebrates found in shallow water and mudflats.
facts.behavior: Flies in tight, fast-wheeling flocks that twist and turn in unison. Males give a distinctive high-pitched whistling 'kreeet' call.

facts.size_shape: A small diving duck, 15-19 inches long with a slim body and a dramatic fan-shaped crest that can be raised or lowered. Thin, serrated bill.
facts.color_pattern: Males have a striking black-and-white fan crest, black head, white breast patches, and chestnut sides. Females are warm brown with a shaggy rusty-orange crest.
facts.where_to_find: Found across North America in wooded swamps, rivers, ponds, and estuaries. Breeds in tree cavities near water; winters across much of the US.
facts.what_they_eat: Small fish, aquatic invertebrates, and crayfish, caught by diving with excellent underwater vision.
facts.behavior: Males raise and lower their spectacular white crest during courtship displays. Nests in tree cavities, sometimes sharing with Wood Ducks. One of the most visually striking ducks in North America.

facts.size_shape: A small, chunky songbird about 5-6 inches long with a short thick curved bill and a slightly notched tail.
facts.color_pattern: Males have a bright red head, breast, and rump with brown-streaked wings and back. Females are plain brown with heavy streaking on the breast and belly.
facts.where_to_find: Originally from the western US, now found across all of North America. Thrives in urban and suburban areas, farms, and forest edges. A very common backyard bird.
facts.what_they_eat: Almost entirely seeds and buds. A frequent visitor to backyard feeders, especially for nyjer and sunflower seeds.
facts.behavior: Sings a long, cheerful, rambling warble year-round. Often seen in flocks at feeders. The reddest males are preferred by females — redness reflects diet quality.

facts.size_shape: A large woodpecker, 11-14 inches long with a long bill and round head. Larger than a robin.
facts.color_pattern: Brown barred back, spotted breast with a black bib, red patch on the back of the head, and a white rump patch visible in flight. Eastern birds have yellow wing linings; western birds have red.
facts.where_to_find: Found across North America in open woodlands, forest edges, parks, and suburbs. One of the most widespread woodpeckers on the continent.
facts.what_they_eat: Ants and beetles are the primary diet, foraged mostly on the ground — unusual for a woodpecker.
facts.behavior: Frequently seen foraging on the ground, using its long sticky tongue to extract ants. Calls loudly with a repeated 'wicka-wicka-wicka' and drums on metal gutters to amplify territorial signals.

facts.size_shape: Medium-sized songbird about 8-9 inches long (similar to a robin). Stocky build with a short, pointed bill and a slightly rounded tail.
facts.color_pattern: Males are glossy jet black with bold red-and-yellow shoulder patches (epaulets) that flash during display. Females are streaky brown overall, resembling a large sparrow.
facts.where_to_find: One of the most abundant birds in North America, found from Alaska to Central America. Thrives in marshes, wetlands, meadows, and along roadsides.
facts.what_they_eat: Mostly seeds and grains, supplemented with insects and berries during summer.
facts.behavior: Males are fiercely territorial and perch conspicuously on cattails or fences, puffing up their shoulder patches and singing a raspy 'konk-la-ree' call. Form massive flocks of millions in winter.

facts.size_shape: A medium-sized gull, 17-21 inches long with a wingspan of about 4 feet. Slender build with a rounded head and medium-length bill.
facts.color_pattern: Clean white head and body, pale gray back and wings, black wingtips with white spots, yellow legs, and a distinctive black ring around the yellow bill.
facts.where_to_find: The most common gull in North America, found year-round across most of the continent near lakes, rivers, parking lots, landfills, and coasts.
facts.what_they_eat: Highly opportunistic — eats fish, insects, earthworms, garbage, and food scraps. Often follows farm equipment for disturbed insects.
facts.behavior: Highly adaptable and bold around humans; commonly seen scavenging in parking lots. Migrates in large flocks along major river corridors.

facts.size_shape: A medium-sized diving duck, 14-18 inches long with a peaked, angular head distinctive in profile and a compact body.
facts.color_pattern: Males have a black back and breast, gray sides with a white shoulder stripe, and a black head with purple iridescence. Females are brown with a white eye ring and white ring near the bill tip.
facts.where_to_find: Breeds in northern boreal lakes and bogs; winters across the southern US, both coasts, and into Central America on freshwater lakes and reservoirs.
facts.what_they_eat: Aquatic plants, seeds, and invertebrates, obtained by diving.
facts.behavior: Despite the name, the chestnut neck ring is almost invisible in the field — look instead for the white ring on the bill and the peaked head shape.

facts.size_shape: A familiar medium-sized songbird, 9-11 inches long with a plump body, long legs, and a medium-length yellow bill. One of the larger North American thrushes.
facts.color_pattern: Dark gray-brown back and wings with a warm orange-red breast. White eye rings, white-streaked throat, and a white belly patch.
facts.where_to_find: Found across North America from Alaska to Mexico in nearly every habitat — lawns, parks, forests, and suburbs. One of the first birds to return in spring.
facts.what_they_eat: Earthworms pulled from the ground, plus berries and fruit in fall and winter.
facts.behavior: Famous for running across lawns and cocking its head to listen for earthworms. Sings a rich, caroling song at dawn and dusk. Forms large flocks in winter to feed on berry-producing trees.

facts.size_shape: A medium-sized dabbling duck, about 17-20 inches long, with a uniquely large spatula-shaped bill that is wider at the tip than the base.
facts.color_pattern: Males have an iridescent green head, white breast, chestnut sides, and a bright blue shoulder patch visible in flight. Females are mottled brown with the same distinctive oversized bill.
facts.where_to_find: Found across North America, breeding in prairie wetlands and wintering along both coasts, in the south, and into Central America. Favors shallow, muddy marshes and ponds.
facts.what_they_eat: Filters tiny invertebrates, seeds, and aquatic plants from the water using comb-like structures along the edges of its bill.
facts.behavior: Often swims in tight circles with other shovelers to stir up food from the bottom. The distinctive shovel-shaped bill makes it one of the easiest ducks to identify at a glance.

facts.size_shape: A medium-sized sparrow, 4.7-6.7 inches long with a round head and a long rounded tail that it pumps up and down in flight.
facts.color_pattern: Brown and white with heavy brown streaking on the breast that converges into a central breast spot. Brown and gray face with a gray eyebrow stripe.
facts.where_to_find: One of the most widespread birds in North America, found from Alaska to Mexico in shrubby areas near water, marshes, roadsides, and gardens.
facts.what_they_eat: Seeds, insects, and berries. Forages on the ground or in low vegetation.
facts.behavior: Males sing persistently from exposed perches with a complex melodious song beginning with three repeated notes. Among the first sparrows to sing in spring.

facts.size_shape: Medium-sized dabbling duck, about 18-23 inches long with a round head and short bill that give it a distinctive compact look compared to other ducks.
facts.color_pattern: Males have a white forehead patch, iridescent green streak behind the eye, pinkish-brown breast, and white belly. Females are mottled warm brown with a grayish head.
facts.where_to_find: Breeds in the prairies and parklands of western Canada and the northern US. Winters widely across both coasts, the South, and into Central America on marshes, lakes, and flooded fields.
facts.what_they_eat: Mainly aquatic plants and grasses. Frequently steals food from diving ducks and coots surfacing with vegetation.
facts.behavior: Usually seen in tight flocks on open water. Males give a distinctive three-note whistling call. Often grazes on land like a goose, unlike most ducks.
